name: voice-ai-engine-development
description: Build real-time conversational AI voice engines using async worker pipelines, streaming transcription, LLM agents, and TTS synthesis with interrupt handling and multi-provider support
capabilities: Build async worker pipeline architecture for voice processing
Create LLM-powered conversational agents with context
Integrate text-to-speech synthesis for voice output
Switch between multiple voice AI providers
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 214 static findings are false positives. The skill is legitimate voice AI development documentation with example code. Detected patterns include: markdown code fences (not shell commands), legitimate audio data representation with \x00, standard network bindings (0.0.0.0), and placeholder configuration strings. No actual security issues found.
valueStatement: Create production-ready conversational AI voice engines with async worker pipelines, streaming transcription, LLM agents, and TTS synthesis. This skill provides complete guidance on building voice assistants with interrupt handling and multi-provider support.
limitations: Does not include actual API keys for voice providers
Audio processing examples use mock data
Does not cover mobile or embedded deployment

useCases.title: Build a Voice Assistant
useCases.description: Create a real-time voice assistant that can listen, process, and respond to user queries with natural speech

useCases.targetUser: Product teams adding voice to apps
useCases.title: Implement Voice Chatbot
useCases.description: Add voice conversation capability to existing chatbots for phone or video call applications

useCases.title: Create Multi-Provider Voice System
useCases.description: Build a flexible voice system that can switch between different transcription and TTS providers

promptTemplates.title: Basic Voice Pipeline
promptTemplates.prompt: Use the voice-ai-engine-development skill to create a simple voice assistant that listens for speech, converts it to text, processes it with an LLM, and speaks the response back
promptTemplates.scenario: Setting up a basic voice AI pipeline

promptTemplates.title: Add Interrupt Handling
promptTemplates.prompt: Using voice-ai-engine-development, show me how to add interrupt handling so users can stop the AI mid-response

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Provider Setup
promptTemplates.prompt: I want to use multiple TTS providers (ElevenLabs, Google, OpenAI) in my voice engine. Show me the factory pattern implementation from voice-ai-engine-development

promptTemplates.title: Optimize Streaming Latency
promptTemplates.prompt: Using voice-ai-engine-development best practices, how do I optimize the async pipeline to minimize latency between user speech and AI response?

bestPractices: Use async worker queues for independent processing stages to enable true concurrency
Buffer LLM responses before TTS to ensure smooth audio output without truncation
Implement interrupt handling early as it affects the entire pipeline architecture
antiPatterns: Processing audio synchronously in a single thread (blocks real-time performance)
Sending incomplete chunks to transcription (causes accuracy issues)
Starting TTS before LLM response is complete (results in cut-off speech)

faq.question: What voice providers does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill covers integration with Deepgram, Google Speech, and Whisper for transcription; OpenAI, Gemini, and Anthropic for LLM; and ElevenLabs, Google Cloud TTS, and OpenAI TTS for voice synthesis.

faq.question: Do I need API keys to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you will need API keys from your chosen providers (Deepgram, OpenAI, ElevenLabs, etc.). The skill provides configuration templates but does not include actual keys.

faq.question: Can this run completely locally?
faq.answer: Some components can run locally (like Whisper transcription), but most production voice AI systems require cloud API calls for LLM processing and high-quality TTS.

faq.question: How does interrupt handling work?
faq.answer: The skill uses an InterruptibleEvent wrapper that tracks whether the user has spoken. The get_message_up_to() method returns only the text spoken before the interruption.

faq.question: What Python libraries are required?
faq.answer: Core dependencies include asyncio, FastAPI, websockets for real-time communication, and provider SDKs. The skill lists all required packages in documentation.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production deployment?
faq.answer: The skill provides architectural patterns suitable for production, but you will need to implement the actual provider integrations and handle scaling considerations.
